title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Oliver Green, who drives a mobile library van.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're talking to Oliver Green, who drives a mobile library van. Oliver, you drive a library van to villages. What attracted you to this job? || Guest: I worked in a town library for several years, and I liked helping readers. But I wanted to meet people who found it hard to travel into town. [20]The idea of taking books to people instead of waiting for them to come to us really appealed to me[/20]. Some villages have no buses after lunch, so the van can make a big difference, especially for older readers and families with small children. || Interviewer: What was difficult when you were starting out? || Guest: Driving the van is enjoyable, but it is not the same as driving a car. It is wide, and some village roads are narrow. At first, [21]I was most nervous about parking the van safely in small spaces[/21]. People were kind and often came out to guide me. After a few weeks, I knew the difficult corners, and now I plan each stop carefully before I set off. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy most about it now? || Guest: Many people ask for crime stories, but the most popular books change with the season. In winter, people want long novels. In summer, families borrow travel guides and books for children. [22]I have to choose a mixture, because the van has much less shelf space than a normal library[/22]. If I take too many of one type, someone will be disappointed, so I check borrowing records every month. || Interviewer: Have you changed the way you work since then? || Guest: The best part is the conversations. A book is often just the start. [23]One regular visitor comes mainly because she enjoys talking about what she has read[/23]. She lives alone and tells me which characters annoyed her or made her laugh. Those chats remind me that libraries are not only about books; they are also about keeping people connected. || Interviewer: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work? || Guest: Technology has changed the job a little. We now use a small tablet to record loans, which is quicker than paper. However, [24]I still bring printed lists for people who do not like using screens[/24]. Some readers enjoy choosing online, but others prefer to hold a list and mark titles with a pencil. I try to offer both, so nobody feels left behind. || Interviewer: And what are your plans for the future? || Guest: Next year the library service wants to add evening stops in two larger villages. [25]I hope this will help teenagers who are at school when the van usually arrives[/25]. They often ask for revision books or graphic novels, but our afternoon times do not suit them. If the evening visits work well, we may add more stops later. || Interviewer: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar? || Guest: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like mobile library van, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal. || Interviewer: Thank you, that was really interesting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're talking to Oliver Green, who drives a mobile library van. Oliver, you drive a library van to villages. What attracted you to this job?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I worked in a town library for several years, and I liked helping readers. But I wanted to meet people who found it hard to travel into town. The idea of taking books to people instead of waiting for them to come to us really appealed to me. Some villages have no buses after lunch, so the van can make a big difference, especially for older readers and families with small children.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was difficult when you were starting out?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Driving the van is enjoyable, but it is not the same as driving a car. It is wide, and some village roads are narrow. At first, I was most nervous about parking the van safely in small spaces. People were kind and often came out to guide me. After a few weeks, I knew the difficult corners, and now I plan each stop carefully before I set off.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy most about it now?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Many people ask for crime stories, but the most popular books change with the season. In winter, people want long novels. In summer, families borrow travel guides and books for children. I have to choose a mixture, because the van has much less shelf space than a normal library. If I take too many of one type, someone will be disappointed, so I check borrowing records every month.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Have you changed the way you work since then?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The best part is the conversations. A book is often just the start. One regular visitor comes mainly because she enjoys talking about what she has read. She lives alone and tells me which characters annoyed her or made her laugh. Those chats remind me that libraries are not only about books; they are also about keeping people connected.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Technology has changed the job a little. We now use a small tablet to record loans, which is quicker than paper. However, I still bring printed lists for people who do not like using screens. Some readers enjoy choosing online, but others prefer to hold a list and mark titles with a pencil. I try to offer both, so nobody feels left behind.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: And what are your plans for the future?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Next year the library service wants to add evening stops in two larger villages. I hope this will help teenagers who are at school when the van usually arrives. They often ask for revision books or graphic novels, but our afternoon times do not suit them. If the evening visits work well, we may add more stops later.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like mobile library van, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Thank you, that was really interesting.

questions.question: Why did Oliver want to drive the library van?

options.A: He disliked working inside the town library.
options.B: He liked bringing books directly to readers.
options.C: He wanted to travel to different cities.

questions.explanation: Oliver says he liked the idea of taking books to people instead of waiting for them to come to the library.

questions.question: What worried Oliver at first?

options.C: parking the van in small places

questions.explanation: Oliver says he was most nervous about parking the van safely in small spaces.

questions.question: Why does Oliver choose books carefully?

options.B: Most readers only want crime stories.

questions.explanation: Oliver explains that the van has much less shelf space than a normal library.

questions.question: What does Oliver say about one regular visitor?

options.A: She borrows more books than anyone else.
options.B: She mainly likes discussing books with him.
options.C: She helps him choose books for the van.

questions.explanation: Oliver says one regular visitor comes mainly because she enjoys talking about what she has read.

questions.question: Why does Oliver still use printed lists?

options.B: They are cheaper than online records.
options.C: Some readers prefer not to use screens.

questions.explanation: Oliver brings printed lists for people who do not like using screens.

questions.question: Who does Oliver think evening stops will help?

options.A: teenagers who are busy during the day

questions.explanation: Oliver hopes evening stops will help teenagers who are at school when the van usually arrives.
